---
title: "Recovery Re-engagement"
format: html
---

# Recovery Re-Engagement Plan

When relapse drift begins<!--reference relapse_drift_reading.qmd-->, the most effective response is quick, deliberate re-engagement with structure and support. Waiting to feel motivated often increases risk. In recovery, action comes first — motivation usually follows.

A strong re-engagement plan is:

- **Time-bound** (has clear deadlines)
- **Behavior-specific** (focuses on observable actions)
- **Realistic and immediate** (can be started within 24–48 hours)



## Use this worksheet to develop a re-engagement plan using the following steps.

- **Step 1: Identify the Drift**. Look honestly at where structure has weakened. Small shifts matter.

- **Step 2: Take Corrective Action**. Choose specific behaviors that restore stability quickly.

- **Step 3: Commit to a Timeline**. Set deadlines within the next **24–72 hours** whenever possible.


### Re-Engagement Plan

| Area | What has drifted? | My corrective action (specific & measurable) | Deadline | Completed|
|------|------------------|--------------------------------------------|----------|-------------|
| Daily schedule |  |  |  |  |
| Support network |  |  |  |  |
| Stress management |  |  |  |  |
| Sleep / physical health |  |  |  |  |


::: callout

## High-Risk Signals to Watch For

- Skipping routines or appointments  
- Isolating from support people  
- Increased stress without coping strategies  
- Changes in sleep, eating, or physical health  

If you notice two or more signals, increase support immediately.

:::


![](https://github.com/jjcurtin/aud_support/blob/main/modules/images/mooring2.jpeg?raw=true){fig-align="center"}


::: callout
### Try This

Treat this as an experiment in regaining control:

- Complete at least 2 corrective actions today
- Notice how your motivation changes *after* taking action
- Write down what worked best so you can reuse it

:::
